title: Sort DLL
description: Sort doubly linked list using merge sort

examples.explanation: Initial list: 4-2-1-3. Pass 1: Compare 4 and 2, swap: 2-4-1-3. Compare 4 and 1, swap: 2-1-4-3. Compare 4 and 3, swap: 2-1-3-4. Swapped = true. Pass 2: Compare 2 and 1, swap: 1-2-3-4. Compare 2 and 3, no swap. Compare 3 and 4, no swap. Swapped = true. Pass 3: Compare 1 and 2, no swap. Compare 2 and 3, no swap. Compare 3 and 4, no swap. Swapped = false. Sorted list: 1-2-3-4.
Initial list: 5-1. Pass 1: Compare 5 and 1, swap: 1-5. Swapped = true. Pass 2: Compare 1 and 5, no swap. Swapped = false. Sorted list: 1-5.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Bubble Sort on DLL
approaches.summary: Repeatedly traverse the doubly linked list, swapping adjacent nodes when they're out of order until the list is sorted. This is the simplest sorting algorithm to implement on a linked list but extremely inefficient for larger lists.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Doubly Linked List Using Bubble Sort
ladders.desc: Implement bubble sort algorithm to sort a doubly linked list in ascending order.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires sorting a doubly linked list using the bubble sort algorithm. The intuition behind bubble sort is simple: repeatedly traverse the list, comparing adjacent nodes and swapping them if they're in the wrong order. Each full pass through the list 'bubbles' the largest unsorted element to its correct position at the end. For a doubly linked list, we need to handle the prev pointers carefully during swaps. The algorithm continues until no swaps are needed in a complete pass, indicating the list is sorted. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize a boolean swapped = true. 2. While swapped is true: a. Set swapped = false. b. Traverse the list from head to tail (or until the last sorted node). c. For each node, compare it with its next node. d. If current node's value > next node's value, swap them and set swapped = true. 3. Return the (potentially new) head of the sorted list. Time complexity is O(n²) in worst and average cases (when list is reverse sorted or random), and O(n) in best case (already sorted). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few pointers. This connects to the parent problem of sorting a DLL, with bubble sort being the simplest but least efficient approach. Common mistakes include: not updating prev pointers correctly during swaps (can break the doubly linked structure), not handling edge cases like empty list or single node, and infinite loops if swapped flag logic is wrong. Real code pattern: def bubbleSortDLL(head): if not head or not head.next: return head; swapped = True; last = None; while swapped: swapped = False; current = head; while current.next != last: if current.val > current.next.val: # Swap nodes temp = current.next; current.next = temp.next; if temp.next: temp.next.prev = current; temp.prev = current.prev; if current.prev: current.prev.next = temp; else: head = temp; current.prev = temp; temp.next = current; swapped = True; else: current = current.next; last = current; return head

examples.trace: Initial list: 4-2-1-3. Pass 1: Compare 4 and 2, swap: 2-4-1-3. Compare 4 and 1, swap: 2-1-4-3. Compare 4 and 3, swap: 2-1-3-4. Swapped = true. Pass 2: Compare 2 and 1, swap: 1-2-3-4. Compare 2 and 3, no swap. Compare 3 and 4, no swap. Swapped = true. Pass 3: Compare 1 and 2, no swap. Compare 2 and 3, no swap. Compare 3 and 4, no swap. Swapped = false. Sorted list: 1-2-3-4.
Initial list: 5-1. Pass 1: Compare 5 and 1, swap: 1-5. Swapped = true. Pass 2: Compare 1 and 5, no swap. Swapped = false. Sorted list: 1-5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Swap Adjacent Nodes in Doubly Linked List
ladders.desc: Implement a function to swap two adjacent nodes in a doubly linked list.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core operation in bubble sort for DLLs. The intuition is that when we find two adjacent nodes out of order, we need to exchange their positions while maintaining the doubly linked structure. This involves updating the next and prev pointers of the nodes themselves and their neighbors. The step-by-step algorithm: Given nodes A and B where A.next == B: 1. Store references: B_next = B.next, A_prev = A.prev. 2. Update A's next to point to B_next. 3. If B_next exists, update B_next.prev to point to A. 4. Update B's prev to point to A_prev. 5. If A_prev exists, update A_prev.next to point to B; else, B becomes the new head. 6. Update B's next to point to A. 7. Update A's prev to point to B. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves constant pointer updates. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the full bubble sort by being called whenever a swap is needed during traversal. Common mistakes include: forgetting to update the prev pointer of the node after B (if it exists), not handling the case where A is the head (A_prev is null), and incorrect order of pointer updates leading to lost references. Real code pattern: def swapAdjacent(head, A, B): if not A or not B or A.next != B: return head; B_next = B.next; A_prev = A.prev; A.next = B_next; if B_next: B_next.prev = A; B.prev = A_prev; if A_prev: A_prev.next = B; else: head = B; B.next = A; A.prev = B; return head;

examples.input: List: 1 <-> 2 <-> 3, swap nodes with values 1 and 2
examples.trace: Initial: A=1, B=2, A_prev=null, B_next=3. Step 1: A.next = 3. Step 2: 3.prev = 1. Step 3: B.prev = null. Step 4: Since A_prev is null, head becomes B (2). Step 5: B.next = 1. Step 6: A.prev = 2. Result: 2-1-3.

examples.input: List: 4 <-> 5 <-> 6 <-> 7, swap nodes with values 5 and 6
examples.trace: Initial: A=5, B=6, A_prev=4, B_next=7. Step 1: A.next = 7. Step 2: 7.prev = 5. Step 3: B.prev = 4. Step 4: 4.next = 6. Step 5: B.next = 5. Step 6: A.prev = 6. Result: 4-6-5-7.

testCases.input: null, swap nodes null and null

ladders.title: Core Logic — Traverse and Compare Adjacent Nodes in DLL
ladders.desc: Implement traversal of a doubly linked list while comparing each node with its next node.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique in bubble sort: iterating through the list and checking if adjacent nodes are in the correct order. The intuition is that by comparing each pair, we can identify where swaps are needed. For bubble sort, we traverse from the head to the tail (or to a stopping point that moves inward as elements get sorted). The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Start at the head node. 2. While current node exists and current.next exists: a. Compare current.val with current.next.val. b. If current.val > current.next.val, we need a swap (handled by the swap sub-routine). c. Move to the next node (current = current.next) if no swap occurred, or stay at the same position if a swap happened (since the current node after swap is the smaller one that moved forward). 3. Continue until the end of the traversal range. Time complexity for one traversal is O(n) where n is the number of nodes in the traversal range. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the key sub-routine by identifying which nodes to swap. Common mistakes include: not updating the current pointer correctly after a swap (can skip nodes or cause infinite loops), not handling the end condition properly (e.g., traversing beyond the tail), and comparing nodes that are not adjacent. Real code pattern: def traverseAndCompare(head): if not head: return; current = head; while current and current.next: if current.val > current.next.val: # Swap needed, call swap function head = swapAdjacent(head, current, current.next); # After swap, current points to the smaller node now in current's position else: current = current.next;

examples.trace: Start at head: current=3. Compare 3 and 1: 3>1, swap needed. After swap, list becomes 1-3-2, current still points to node with value 1? Actually after swap, the smaller node (1) moves to current's position, so we compare next: current=1, next=3? Wait, need to adjust: In bubble sort traversal, after swap, we might stay or move; typically we move to next. Let's trace properly: Initial: 3-1-2. current=3, compare 3 and 1, swap to 1-3-2. Now current should be at node 1? But in code, after swap, current might point to the smaller node (1), then we move to next: current=1.next which is 3. Compare 3 and 2, swap to 1-2-3. End traversal.
Start at head: current=1. Compare 1 and 2: 1<2, no swap, move to current=2. Compare 2 and 3: 2<3, no swap, move to current=3. current.next is null, end traversal.

testCases.expected: swap at nodes 3 and 2, then 2 and 1
swap at nodes 4 and 3, then 3 and 2
swap at nodes 6 and 5, 5 and 4, 4 and 3
swap at nodes 10 and 9, 9 and 8, 8 and 7

ladders.title: Building Block — Traverse Doubly Linked List
ladders.desc: Implement a function to traverse a doubly linked list from head to tail.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental for bubble sort, as we need to iterate through the list multiple times. The intuition is that a doubly linked list allows traversal in both directions, but for bubble sort, we typically go forward from head to tail. Traversal involves starting at the head node and following the next pointers until we reach null. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize a pointer current = head. 2. While current is not null: a. Process the current node (e.g., print its value, compare with next). b. Move current to current.next. 3. End when current becomes null. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n nodes. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the core logic by providing the mechanism to access each node during comparison. Common mistakes include: not checking for empty list (head is null), infinite loops if next pointers are corrupted, and off-by-one errors when accessing current.next. Real code pattern: def traverseDLL(head): current = head; while current: # Do something with current.val current = current.next;

examples.trace: Start: current = node with value 1. Process node 1. Move current to node 1.next which is node 2. Process node 2. Move current to node 2.next which is node 3. Process node 3. Move current to node 3.next which is null. End.
Start: current = node with value 5. Process node 5. Move current to node 5.next which is null. End.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Next and Previous Nodes in DLL
ladders.desc: Understand and implement accessing the next and previous nodes from a given node in a doubly linked list.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is crucial for bubble sort because we need to compare and swap adjacent nodes, which requires accessing both next and prev pointers. The intuition is that each node in a doubly linked list stores references to its next node and previous node, allowing bidirectional traversal. To access the next node, use node.next; to access the previous node, use node.prev. If node.next is null, it means the node is the tail; if node.prev is null, it means the node is the head. The step-by-step algorithm: Given a node N: 1. Next node: if N.next exists, it points to the next node; else, N is the last node. 2. Previous node: if N.prev exists, it points to the previous node; else, N is the first node. Time complexity is O(1) for each access. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the building block of traversal by enabling movement through the list. Common mistakes include: not checking for null before accessing node.next or node.prev (can cause null pointer errors), and misunderstanding that node.prev might be null for the head. Real code pattern: def accessNeighbors(node): if node: next_node = node.next; prev_node = node.prev; # next_node and prev_node may be null

examples.input: List: 1 <-> 2 <-> 3, access from node with value 2
examples.trace: Node with value 2: node.next points to node with value 3, so next is 3. node.prev points to node with value 1, so prev is 1.

examples.input: List: 4 <-> 5, access from node with value 4
examples.trace: Node with value 4: node.next points to node with value 5, so next is 5. node.prev is null since it's the head, so prev is null.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Node Structure in Doubly Linked List
ladders.desc: Understand and define the basic node structure for a doubly linked list.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a doubly linked list is composed of nodes, where each node contains data and pointers to both the next and previous nodes. The intuition is that this structure allows efficient traversal in both directions, unlike a singly linked list. Each node typically has three fields: val (the data value), next (pointer to the next node), and prev (pointer to the previous node). In many programming languages, this is implemented as a class or struct. The step-by-step definition: 1. Define a class Node with attributes: val, next, prev. 2. Initialize a node with a given value, setting next and prev to null initially. 3. Nodes are linked by updating next and prev pointers. Time and space analysis: Creating a node takes O(1) time and O(1) space per node. This feeds into the basic operation by providing the objects we access. Common mistakes include: forgetting to initialize prev pointer (should be null for new nodes), not updating both next and prev when linking nodes, and memory leaks if pointers are not managed properly. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.next = None; self.prev = None;

examples.trace: Initialize Node object: set val to 10, next to None, prev to None.

examples.input: Create two nodes with values 20 and 30, link them as 20 <-> 30
examples.trace: Create nodeA with val=20. Create nodeB with val=30. Set nodeA.next = nodeB. Set nodeB.prev = nodeA. nodeA.prev remains null, nodeB.next remains null.

testCases.expected: node1: val=1, next=node2, prev=null; node2: val=2, next=null, prev=node1
node3: val=3, next=node4, prev=null; node4: val=4, next=null, prev=node3
node5: val=5, next=node6, prev=null; node6: val=6, next=node7, prev=node5; node7: val=7, next=null, prev=node6

testCases.input: Create node with value 8, no linking
Create node with value 0, link to itself? Not typical
Create node with value 10, set next to null explicitly

approaches.name: Approach 2: Top-Down Merge Sort
approaches.summary: Recursively split the doubly linked list into halves using a slow/fast pointer technique to find the middle, then merge the sorted halves back together while maintaining the prev/next pointers. This is the classic merge sort adaptation for linked lists.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Doubly Linked List Using Top-Down Merge Sort
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of merge sort for a doubly linked list using recursive splitting and merging.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution to sort a doubly linked list using the top-down merge sort approach. The intuition is that merge sort's divide-and-conquer nature works perfectly for linked lists since we don't need random access - we can split lists by traversing pointers. The algorithm works by recursively splitting the list into halves until we reach single nodes (which are trivially sorted), then merging the sorted halves back together while maintaining both next and prev pointers. The key insight is that we can find the middle of a linked list using the slow/fast pointer technique without needing to know the length in advance. For the merge step, we need to carefully update both next and prev pointers to maintain the doubly linked structure. The time complexity is O(n log n) because we split the list log n times and merge n elements each time. Space complexity is O(log n) for the recursion stack. This connects to L1 by using both the split and merge helper functions. Common mistakes include not properly updating prev pointers during merge, not handling the base case correctly in recursion, and creating cycles by incorrectly linking nodes. Real code pattern: def mergeSort(head): if not head or not head.next: return head; mid = findMiddle(head); left = mergeSort(head); right = mergeSort(mid); return merge(left, right);

examples.trace: Step 1: Call mergeSort on head (4). List has more than 1 node, so find middle. Slow=4, fast=4; slow moves to 2, fast moves to 1; slow moves to 1, fast moves to null. Middle is node 1. Split into left: 4<->2 and right: 1<->3. Step 2: Recursively sort left (4<->2). Find middle: slow=4, fast=4; slow moves to 2, fast moves to null. Middle is 2. Split into left: 4 and right: 2. Both are single nodes (base case). Merge 4 and 2: Compare 4 and 2, 2 is smaller. Result: 2<->4 with prev pointers set. Step 3: Recursively sort right (1<->3). Find middle: slow=1, fast=1; slow moves to 3, fast moves to null. Middle is 3. Split into left: 1 and right: 3. Merge 1 and 3: Compare 1 and 3, 1 is smaller. Result: 1<->3. Step 4: Merge sorted left (2<->4) and right (1<->3). Initialize result pointer. Compare 2 and 1: 1 smaller, add 1. Compare 2 and 3: 2 smaller, add 2. Compare 4 and 3: 3 smaller, add 3. Add remaining 4. Final: 1<->2<->3<->4.

Step 1: Call mergeSort on head (5). List has 2 nodes, find middle. Slow=5, fast=5; slow moves to 1, fast moves to null. Middle is node 1. Split into left: 5 and right: 1. Step 2: Both are single nodes (base case). Step 3: Merge 5 and 1. Compare 5 and 1: 1 is smaller. Set result to 1. Connect 1.next = 5, 5.prev = 1. Return head 1. Final: 1<->5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Sorted Doubly Linked Lists
ladders.desc: Merge two sorted doubly linked lists into one sorted list while maintaining prev pointers.
ladders.explanation: This is the most critical helper function in the merge sort algorithm. The intuition is that we can merge two sorted lists by comparing their front elements repeatedly, taking the smaller element each time, and appending it to our result list. For doubly linked lists, we must not only set the next pointers but also the prev pointers to maintain the bidirectional links. The algorithm uses a dummy node to simplify edge cases, then iterates through both lists comparing current nodes. When we take a node from one list, we update its prev pointer to point to the last node in our result list. The time complexity is O(n+m) where n and m are the lengths of the two lists. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few pointers. This connects to L0 as it's the core merging operation in the full merge sort. Common mistakes include forgetting to update prev pointers, not handling the remaining nodes after the main loop, and creating cycles by incorrect linking. Real code pattern: def merge(left, right): dummy = Node(0); tail = dummy; while left and right: if left.val <= right.val: tail.next = left; left.prev = tail; left = left.next; else: tail.next = right; right.prev = tail; right = right.next; tail = tail.next; tail.next = left or right; if tail.next: tail.next.prev = tail; return dummy.next;

examples.trace: Step 1: Create dummy node. tail = dummy. Step 2: Compare left(2) and right(1). 1 is smaller. tail.next = right(1). Set 1.prev = dummy. tail = 1. right moves to 3. Step 3: Compare left(2) and right(3). 2 is smaller. tail.next = left(2). Set 2.prev = 1. tail = 2. left moves to 4. Step 4: Compare left(4) and right(3). 3 is smaller. tail.next = right(3). Set 3.prev = 2. tail = 3. right becomes null. Step 5: Attach remaining left(4). tail.next = 4. Set 4.prev = 3. Return dummy.next which is 1.

Step 1: Create dummy node. tail = dummy. Step 2: Compare left(1) and right(2). 1 is smaller. tail.next = left(1). Set 1.prev = dummy. tail = 1. left moves to 5. Step 3: Compare left(5) and right(2). 2 is smaller. tail.next = right(2). Set 2.prev = 1. tail = 2. right moves to 3. Step 4: Compare left(5) and right(3). 3 is smaller. tail.next = right(3). Set 3.prev = 2. tail = 3. right moves to 4. Step 5: Compare left(5) and right(4). 4 is smaller. tail.next = right(4). Set 4.prev = 3. tail = 4. right becomes null. Step 6: Attach remaining left(5). tail.next = 5. Set 5.prev = 4. Return dummy.next which is 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Find Middle of Doubly Linked List Using Slow/Fast Pointers
ladders.desc: Split a doubly linked list into two halves by finding the middle node.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique for implementing merge sort on linked lists. The intuition is that we need to split the list into halves for divide-and-conquer, but we can't directly access the middle element like in arrays. The slow/fast pointer technique solves this: we move one pointer (slow) one step at a time and another pointer (fast) two steps at a time. When fast reaches the end, slow will be at the middle. For doubly linked lists, we also need to disconnect the two halves by setting the prev pointer of the middle node to null. The algorithm initializes both pointers at the head, then advances slow by one and fast by two until fast can't move further. We then disconnect at slow. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the list once. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as it's used to split lists before merging. Common mistakes include not handling even/odd length lists correctly, not disconnecting the prev pointer of the middle node, and infinite loops with fast pointer movement. Real code pattern: def findMiddle(head): if not head: return None; slow = head; fast = head; while fast.next and fast.next.next: slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next; mid = slow.next; slow.next = None; if mid: mid.prev = None; return mid;

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize slow=1, fast=1. Step 2: fast.next exists (2), fast.next.next exists (3). Move slow to 2. Move fast to 3. Step 3: fast.next exists (4), fast.next.next is null. Loop ends. slow is at 2. mid = slow.next = 3. Step 4: Disconnect: set slow.next = None (2.next = null). Set mid.prev = None (3.prev = null). Result: left half is 1<->2, right half is 3<->4.

Step 1: Initialize slow=1, fast=1. Step 2: fast.next exists (2), fast.next.next exists (3). Move slow to 2. Move fast to 3. Step 3: fast.next exists (4), fast.next.next exists (5). Move slow to 3. Move fast to 5. Step 4: fast.next is null. Loop ends. slow is at 3. mid = slow.next = 4. Step 5: Disconnect: set slow.next = None (3.next = null). Set mid.prev = None (4.prev = null). Result: left half is 1<->2<->3, right half is 4<->5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Traverse and Manipulate Doubly Linked List Nodes
ladders.desc: Perform basic operations on doubly linked list nodes including traversal and pointer updates.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves understanding how to work with doubly linked list nodes, which have both next and prev pointers. The intuition is that each node points to both its successor and predecessor, allowing bidirectional traversal. Key operations include: traversing forward using next pointers, traversing backward using prev pointers, inserting nodes (which requires updating both next and prev pointers of adjacent nodes), and deleting nodes (which requires updating pointers of both neighbors). For merge sort, we need to traverse to find the middle and manipulate pointers during splitting and merging. The algorithm for traversal is straightforward: start at head, follow next pointers until null. For insertion between nodes A and B: newNode.next = B; newNode.prev = A; if A: A.next = newNode; if B: B.prev = newNode. Time complexity for traversal is O(n). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as it's fundamental to implementing the slow/fast pointer technique. Common mistakes include forgetting to update both next and prev pointers, creating cycles by incorrect linking, and null pointer errors when accessing prev of head or next of tail. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.next = None; self.prev = None; def traverse(head): curr = head; while curr: print(curr.val); curr = curr.next;

examples.input: Insert node 3 between 2 and 4 in list 1<->2<->4<->5
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with list: 1<->2<->4<->5. Node 2 has next=4, Node 4 has prev=2. Step 2: Create new node 3. Step 3: Set 3.next = 4. Step 4: Set 3.prev = 2. Step 5: Update 2.next = 3. Step 6: Update 4.prev = 3. Step 7: Verify: 1.next=2, 2.prev=1, 2.next=3, 3.prev=2, 3.next=4, 4.prev=3, 4.next=5, 5.prev=4.

Step 1: Start with list: 1<->2<->3<->4. Node 2 has next=3, Node 3 has prev=2 and next=4, Node 4 has prev=3. Step 2: To delete node 3, update 2.next = 4. Step 3: Update 4.prev = 2. Step 4: Node 3 is now disconnected (its next and prev can be set to null). Step 5: Verify: 1.next=2, 2.prev=1, 2.next=4, 4.prev=2, 4.next=null.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Swap Nodes in Linked List
ladders.desc: Compare values of nodes and rearrange their links in a linked list.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves comparing node values and changing pointer connections between nodes. The intuition is that sorting algorithms need to compare elements and rearrange them based on those comparisons. For linked lists, we can't swap values directly like in arrays; instead, we need to rearrange the pointers. The basic operation is: given two nodes A and B, if A.val > B.val, we need to reposition B before A by updating the next/prev pointers of A, B, and their neighbors. This involves: storing references to neighbors, breaking existing links, and creating new links in the correct order. For doubly linked lists, we must update both next and prev pointers. Time complexity is O(1) for a single comparison and pointer update. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used when manipulating nodes during sorting. Common mistakes include losing references to nodes during pointer updates, not handling edge cases (head/tail nodes), and creating cycles. Real code pattern: def compareAndRearrange(node1, node2): if node1.val > node2.val: # Need to put node2 before node1 prev1 = node1.prev; next2 = node2.next; if prev1: prev1.next = node2; node2.prev = prev1; node2.next = node1; node1.prev = node2; node1.next = next2; if next2: next2.prev = node1;

examples.trace: Step 1: Initial list: 1<->3<->2<->4. Compare node 3 (value 3) and node 2 (value 2). 3 > 2, so need to put 2 before 3. Step 2: Store references: prev1 = 1 (3's prev), next2 = 4 (2's next). Step 3: Update 1.next = 2 (since prev1 exists). Step 4: Update 2.prev = 1. Step 5: Update 2.next = 3. Step 6: Update 3.prev = 2. Step 7: Update 3.next = 4. Step 8: Update 4.prev = 3. Step 9: Result: 1<->2<->3<->4.

Step 1: Initial list: 5<->1. Compare node 5 (value 5) and node 1 (value 1). 5 > 1, so need to put 1 before 5. Step 2: Store references: prev1 = null (5's prev), next2 = null (1's next). Step 3: Since prev1 is null, we're at head. New head becomes 1. Step 4: Update 1.prev = null. Step 5: Update 1.next = 5. Step 6: Update 5.prev = 1. Step 7: Update 5.next = null. Step 8: Result: 1<->5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Doubly Linked List Node Structure and Pointers
ladders.desc: Understand the basic structure of a doubly linked list node and how pointers connect nodes.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what a doubly linked list node is and how pointers work. The intuition is that a doubly linked list is a sequence of nodes where each node contains data and two pointers - one pointing to the next node (next) and one pointing to the previous node (prev). This allows traversal in both directions. The head node has prev = null, and the tail node has next = null. Each node is like a train car with connections to both the car in front and behind. Creating a node involves allocating memory and setting its value and pointers. Connecting nodes involves setting the next pointer of one node to point to another node, and setting that other node's prev pointer back to the first node. Time complexity for accessing a node is O(n) as we may need to traverse. Space complexity is O(1) per node. This connects to L4 as it's the foundation for all node operations. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize pointers to null, confusing next and prev directions, and not understanding that pointers are references to other nodes. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.next = None; self.prev = None; node1 = Node(1); node2 = Node(2); node1.next = node2; node2.prev = node1;

examples.input: Create two nodes with values 1 and 2, link them doubly
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node1 with val=1. node1.next = None, node1.prev = None. Step 2: Create node2 with val=2. node2.next = None, node2.prev = None. Step 3: Link node1 to node2: set node1.next = node2. Step 4: Link node2 back to node1: set node2.prev = node1. Step 5: Verify: node1.val=1, node1.next points to node2, node1.prev=null. node2.val=2, node2.next=null, node2.prev points to node1.

examples.input: Create three nodes 1, 2, 3 and link them in order
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node1 (1), node2 (2), node3 (3). Step 2: Link node1 to node2: node1.next = node2. Step 3: Link node2 to node1: node2.prev = node1. Step 4: Link node2 to node3: node2.next = node3. Step 5: Link node3 to node2: node3.prev = node2. Step 6: Verify: Traverse forward: node1->node2->node3. Traverse backward: node3->node2->node1.

testCases.input: What is prev of head node?
What is next of tail node?
Access value of second node in 1<->2<->3
How many pointers in a doubly linked list node?

approaches.name: Approach 3: Bottom-Up Merge Sort
approaches.summary: Iteratively merge sorted sublists of increasing size (starting with 1-element lists) by traversing the list and merging pairs without recursion. This avoids recursion overhead and is often more cache-friendly in practice.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Doubly Linked List Using Bottom-Up Merge Sort
ladders.desc: Complete iterative merge sort implementation for doubly linked list without recursion
ladders.explanation: Bottom-up merge sort for doubly linked lists is an iterative approach that sorts by merging increasingly larger sorted sublists. The intuition is to treat each node as a sorted list of size 1, then repeatedly merge adjacent pairs of sorted sublists, doubling the sublist size each pass until the entire list is sorted. This avoids recursion overhead and can be more cache-friendly. The algorithm works by: 1) Initialize sublist size to 1. 2) For each pass through the list, merge adjacent sublists of current size. 3) Double the sublist size after each pass. 4) Continue until sublist size >= total length. The key insight is that after k passes, we have sorted sublists of size 2^k, and merging them preserves the sorted order. Time complexity is O(n log n) because we do log n passes, each taking O(n) time. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few pointers. Connection to L1: This uses the mergeTwoSortedLists function repeatedly. Common mistakes include: not properly updating prev pointers in merged lists, not handling the tail correctly when merging, and infinite loops when sublist size calculation is wrong. Real code pattern: while subSize < n: current = head; prevTail = None; while current: left = current; right = split(left, subSize); nextCurrent = split(right, subSize); mergedHead, mergedTail = merge(left, right); if prevTail: prevTail.next = mergedHead; mergedHead.prev = prevTail; else: newHead = mergedHead; prevTail = mergedTail; current = nextCurrent; subSize *= 2; return newHead.

examples.trace: Initial: 3-1-4-2, size=1. Pass 1: Merge [3] and [1] → [1,3], merge [4] and [2] → [2,4]. List: 1-3-2-4. Size=2. Pass 2: Merge [1,3] and [2,4] → [1,2,3,4]. List: 1-2-3-4. Size=4 ≥ n=4, done.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Two Sorted Doubly Linked Lists
ladders.desc: Merge two sorted DLLs into one sorted DLL while maintaining prev/next pointers
ladders.explanation: This function merges two already sorted doubly linked lists into a single sorted list. The intuition is similar to merging in arrays but with pointer manipulation. We compare nodes from both lists, attach the smaller one to the result, and advance in that list. The key is to correctly update both next and prev pointers to maintain the doubly linked structure. Algorithm: 1) Create dummy node to simplify edge cases. 2) Use pointer 'tail' to track end of merged list. 3) While both lists have nodes: compare current nodes, attach smaller to tail.next, update its prev to tail, advance in that list, move tail forward. 4) Attach remaining nodes from either list. 5) Update prev of first actual node to None. 6) Return merged head (dummy.next). Time: O(m+n) where m,n are list lengths. Space: O(1) using only pointers. Connection to L0: This is called repeatedly in the main loop. Common mistakes: forgetting to set prev pointers, not handling empty lists, incorrect tail updates. Real code pattern: dummy = Node(0); tail = dummy; while l1 and l2: if l1.val <= l2.val: tail.next = l1; l1.prev = tail; l1 = l1.next; else: tail.next = l2; l2.prev = tail; l2 = l2.next; tail = tail.next; tail.next = l1 or l2; if tail.next: tail.next.prev = tail; merged = dummy.next; if merged: merged.prev = None; return merged.

examples.trace: Dummy→. Compare 1 and 2: attach 1, tail→1. Compare 3 and 2: attach 2, tail→1-2. Compare 3 and 4: attach 3, tail→1-2-3. Attach remaining 4: tail→1-2-3-4. Set prevs: 1.prev=None, 2.prev=1, 3.prev=2, 4.prev=3.

Dummy→. Compare 5 and 1: attach 1, tail→1. Compare 5 and 6: attach 5, tail→1-5. Compare 7 and 6: attach 6, tail→1-5-6. Attach remaining 7: tail→1-5-6-7. Set prevs: 1.prev=None, 5.prev=1, 6.prev=5, 7.prev=6.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Split Doubly Linked List into Sublists of Given Size
ladders.desc: Split a DLL into two parts: first 'size' nodes, and the rest, disconnecting them properly
ladders.explanation: This function splits a doubly linked list at a specific position to isolate sublists for merging. The intuition is to traverse 'size' nodes, then disconnect the next pointer and update prev pointers to separate the lists. This is crucial for bottom-up merge sort where we need to process fixed-size chunks. Algorithm: 1) Start from given node 'start'. 2) Traverse 'size-1' nodes (or until end). 3) If reached end, return None as second part. 4) Otherwise, set 'nextStart' to current.next. 5) Disconnect: current.next = None; if nextStart: nextStart.prev = None. 6) Return nextStart as start of second sublist. Time: O(size) to traverse. Space: O(1). Connection to L1: Used before merging to get two sublists. Common mistakes: not updating prev pointer of the new head, off-by-one in traversal, not handling case when size exceeds list length. Real code pattern: current = start; for i in range(size-1): if not current.next: break; current = current.next; nextStart = current.next; current.next = None; if nextStart: nextStart.prev = None; return nextStart.

examples.trace: Start at 1. Traverse 1 node to 2. nextStart = 3. Disconnect: 2.next = None, 3.prev = None. Return nextStart=3.
Start at 5. Traverse 1 node to 6 (size-1=2 but only 1 node left). nextStart = None. Disconnect: 6.next = None. Return nextStart=None.

ladders.title: Building Block — Traverse and Count Nodes in Doubly Linked List
ladders.desc: Count number of nodes in a DLL by traversing from head to tail
ladders.explanation: This function calculates the length of a doubly linked list, which is needed to determine when to stop the merge sort passes. The intuition is simple: start from head, follow next pointers until null, incrementing a counter. For doubly linked lists, we could also traverse backwards from tail, but forward traversal is standard. Algorithm: 1) Initialize count = 0. 2) Start at head. 3) While current is not null: increment count, move to current.next. 4) Return count. Time: O(n) linear traversal. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: Used to get total length for controlling merge passes. Common mistakes: not handling empty list (head=None), infinite loop if circular, off-by-one errors. Real code pattern: count = 0; current = head; while current: count += 1; current = current.next; return count.

examples.trace: Start at 3: count=1. Move to 1: count=2. Move to 4: count=3. Move to null: stop. Return 3.
Head is null, so count remains 0. Return 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Insert Node into Sorted Doubly Linked List
ladders.desc: Insert a single node into already sorted DLL while maintaining sorted order
ladders.explanation: This operation inserts a new node into a sorted doubly linked list at the correct position to preserve ascending order. The intuition is to traverse the list to find the first node with value greater than the new node's value, then insert before it. For doubly linked lists, we need to update both next and prev pointers of adjacent nodes. Algorithm: 1) Handle empty list: new node becomes head. 2) If new node value < head value: insert at front. 3) Else traverse until current.next is null or current.next.value > new value. 4) Insert between current and current.next: new.next = current.next; new.prev = current; if current.next: current.next.prev = new; current.next = new. Time: O(n) worst-case. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: Demonstrates pointer manipulation in sorted context. Common mistakes: not updating prev pointers, edge cases at head/tail, forgetting to handle equal values. Real code pattern: if not head: return new; if new.val < head.val: new.next = head; head.prev = new; return new; current = head; while current.next and current.next.val <= new.val: current = current.next; new.next = current.next; new.prev = current; if current.next: current.next.prev = new; current.next = new; return head.

examples.trace: Start at 1. 2 > 1, move to 3. 2 < 3, so insert between 1 and 3: new.next=3, new.prev=1, 3.prev=2, 1.next=2.
4 < head(5), so insert at front: new.next=5, 5.prev=4, head becomes 4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Doubly Linked List Node Structure and Basic Traversal
ladders.desc: Understand DLL node structure with prev/next pointers and basic forward/backward traversal
ladders.explanation: A doubly linked list (DLL) is a linear data structure where each node contains data and two pointers: 'next' points to the following node, and 'prev' points to the previous node. This allows traversal in both directions. The intuition is that it's like a chain where each link knows its neighbors on both sides. Basic structure: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.prev = None; self.next = None. To traverse forward: start at head, while current: process current.val, current = current.next. To traverse backward: start at tail, while current: process current.val, current = current.prev. Key concepts: head has prev=None, tail has next=None. Time for traversal: O(n). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This is the fundamental structure used in all operations. Common mistakes: not initializing prev/next to None, circular references, accessing null pointers. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.prev = None; self.next = None. Forward traversal: current = head; while current: print(current.val); current = current.next.

examples.trace: Forward: start at 1, print 1, move to 2, print 2, move to 3, print 3, move to null stop. Backward: start at 3, print 3, move to 2, print 2, move to 1, print 1, move to null stop.
Forward: start at 5, print 5, move to null stop. Backward: start at 5, print 5, move to null stop.
